narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and an LSAT score of 32.50, your academic profile places you in the middle of the pack compared to others who've applied, but the law school admission model focuses on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your overall profile and estimated a 49.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection since the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and avoid high failure risks. This prediction wasn't random—it hinged on a few key aspects of your background that stood out as the strongest influences. Your undergraduate GPA emerged as the most significant factor, actually working in your favor by suggesting a stronger likelihood of passing than the model might expect for someone with lower grades, pulling the prediction away from failure. However, your family's upper-middle income bracket played a notable role in the opposite direction, nudging the model toward predicting failure, perhaps because it correlates with certain patterns in the data where similar students faced challenges. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—below the typical 5.53 average—also contributed toward the failure prediction, indicating your performance relative to peers in that later year was a concern that tipped the scales. Compared to typical students, where LSAT averages hover around 36.70 and undergraduate GPAs around 3.21, your full-time student status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much either way. These top three elements—your GPA helping you, but income and Year 3 decile pushing against—collectively outweighed other details like your Year 1 decile of 4 or LSAT, leading to the rejection. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially as a dedicated full-time student from a stable background aiming for law school. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Many applicants in similar situations improve their profiles and succeed on a second application—persistence often pays off here.
